articles.title: HI-PEITHO: More negative data on invasive therapy for higher risk PEs
articles.takeaway: For intermediate-risk PE, do not feel compelled to initiate catheter-directed fibrinolysis based on HI-PEITHO; the apparent benefit seems linked more to clinical instability than direct lysis efficacy. A cautious 'wait and see' approach, closely monitoring cardiorespiratory status, appears safer than proceeding with invasive thrombolysis unless clear indications emerge.
articles.shortSummary: The HI-PEITHO trial examined whether ultrasound-guided, catheter-directed fibrinolysis offered an advantage over standard anticoagulation alone in patients presenting with intermediate-risk pulmonary embolism.
articles.summary: The HI-PEITHO trial examined whether ultrasound-guided, catheter-directed fibrinolysis offered an advantage over standard anticoagulation alone in patients presenting with intermediate-risk pulmonary embolism. While the primary composite endpoint appeared to favor the intervention group, the author cautions against interpreting this as definitive proof of benefit. The observed difference was largely attributed to changes in cardiorespiratory status, specifically those captured by the NEWS score, rather than a direct therapeutic effect of the fibrinolytic agent itself. This suggests that the apparent positive signal might reflect natural disease progression or management adjustments rather than superior intervention efficacy. Overall, the data warrants caution when considering invasive thrombolysis for this patient cohort.

articles.title: Design Refinements for Sepsis Albumin Trials: Building on ICARUS-ED Experience
articles.takeaway: The successful execution of this trial suggests that enrolling patients with undifferentiated sepsis in a real-time, ED-based manner is feasible for future research. When considering albumin administration in the setting of suspected sepsis, remember that these data come from a pilot study and should not dictate immediate practice changes. However, it reinforces the utility of including ward-based patients to better capture the spectrum of early resuscitation needs.
articles.shortSummary: This piece reviews the ICARUS-ED pilot randomized controlled trial, which was designed to test concentrated albumin as an intervention for undifferentiated sepsis managed within the emergency department setting.
articles.summary: This piece reviews the ICARUS-ED pilot randomized controlled trial, which was designed to test concentrated albumin as an intervention for undifferentiated sepsis managed within the emergency department setting. The authors highlight several methodological achievements from this single-center study, most notably demonstrating the feasibility of conducting real-time sepsis trial enrollment. A significant practical point is that they successfully enrolled and managed patients presenting in the ED spectrum, including those who were already admitted to a ward rather than strictly being ICU candidates. Furthermore, the cohort was robustly characterized, with the team confirming an infection diagnosis in 95% of all participants despite the inherent diagnostic uncertainty common during early sepsis presentation.

articles.title: Airway management with a supraglottic airway device during resuscitation by basic life support providers: a systematic review
articles.takeaway: For BLS providers managing cardiac arrest, current evidence does not support routine adoption of an SGA over standard BVM ventilation. Stick with what you know and what is proven effective for the primary rescuer role unless advanced airway management is immediately available. Be cautious interpreting any single study suggesting otherwise due to noted heterogeneity.
articles.shortSummary: This systematic review synthesized the current evidence regarding the utility of supraglottic airway devices (SGA) when utilized by basic life support (BLS)-trained personnel during resuscitation efforts, comparing them…
articles.summary: This systematic review synthesized the current evidence regarding the utility of supraglottic airway devices (SGA) when utilized by basic life support (BLS)-trained personnel during resuscitation efforts, comparing them directly to standard bag-valve mask (BVM) ventilation. The authors concluded that, based on the pooled data, there is no demonstrable benefit to using an SGA over a BVM for this specific group of providers. They highlighted significant heterogeneity across the included studies and noted that comparisons even between different types of SGAs were not feasible due to data limitations. Furthermore, the review pointed out a notable absence of robust data specifically addressing first rescuers or initial first responders in these resuscitation scenarios.

articles.title: JC: Prehospital resuscitative endovascular balloon occlusion of the aorta (REBOA) in non-traumatic out of hospital cardiac arrest: The REBOARREST Trial
articles.takeaway: For non-traumatic out-of-hospital cardiac arrest, current evidence from the REBOARREST trial does not support routinely adding prehospital REBOA to standard ALS protocols. While the technique appears feasible with a two-person team, focus should remain on optimizing core components of advanced resuscitation rather than adopting this intervention based on these findings.
articles.shortSummary: This piece reviews the REBOARREST trial, a randomized controlled trial that assessed whether performing prehospital resuscitative endovascular balloon occlusion of the aorta (REBOA) improves outcomes for patients experi…
articles.summary: This piece reviews the REBOARREST trial, a randomized controlled trial that assessed whether performing prehospital resuscitative endovascular balloon occlusion of the aorta (REBOA) improves outcomes for patients experiencing non-traumatic out-of-hospital cardiac arrest compared to standard advanced life support (ALS). The authors highlight that while the technical feasibility and safety profile of deploying REBOA in the field were demonstrated, the primary endpoint—sustained Return of Spontaneous Circulation (ROSC)—did not show a statistically significant improvement when comparing REBOA versus ALS alone. Overall, the data suggest that integrating this advanced intervention into routine prehospital care may not yield superior survival rates.

articles.title: Comparison of short-term outcomes in open versus endovascular management of traumatic axillosubclavian arterial injuries in the contemporary era: a systematic review and meta-analysis
articles.takeaway: When managing traumatic axillosubclavian injuries, favor endovascular repair if it presents a lower short-term mortality risk compared to open surgery, as suggested here. Remember that while ER appears superior for immediate survival, the rates of major complications like stroke or amputation were similar across both techniques. Given the reliance on retrospective data, these findings should guide initial management decisions but do not replace the need for prospective validation.
articles.shortSummary: This systematic review and meta-analysis directly compared short-term outcomes when managing traumatic injuries to the axillosubclavian artery using either open surgical techniques or endovascular repair (ER).
articles.summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis directly compared short-term outcomes when managing traumatic injuries to the axillosubclavian artery using either open surgical techniques or endovascular repair (ER). The authors synthesized data to determine if one approach offered a survival advantage over the other in this complex trauma setting. The primary conclusion highlighted that ER was associated with lower rates of short-term mortality compared to traditional open surgery. However, it also noted that key adverse outcomes like amputation, thrombosis, and stroke rates appeared comparable between the two management strategies. It is crucial to remember that these findings are derived from retrospective studies, which inherently carry limitations regarding potential confounding variables.

articles.title: Understanding patients with mechanical circulatory support for cardiogenic shock: The role of preceding cardiac arrest
articles.takeaway: Don't let the prior occurrence of OHCA be an absolute contraindication for initiating MCS or enrolling patients in relevant trials, as 30-day mortality rates were similar across groups. However, remember that the risk factors predicting death are distinct between those who had an index OHCA versus those whose shock etiology was different. Always assess the full clinical picture rather than relying solely on the arrest history.
articles.shortSummary: This review compared outcomes in patients requiring mechanical circulatory support (MCS) for cardiogenic shock, specifically stratifying based on whether they had experienced a preceding out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (…
articles.summary: This review compared outcomes in patients requiring mechanical circulatory support (MCS) for cardiogenic shock, specifically stratifying based on whether they had experienced a preceding out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (OHCA). The key finding is that while the overall 30-day mortality rates were comparable between the OHCA and non-OHCA groups, the specific predictors driving death differed significantly between the two cohorts. This suggests that the underlying pathophysiology or comorbidities associated with an index OHCA event might influence prognosis differently than other causes of cardiogenic shock requiring MCS. The authors emphasize that simply having a history of OHCA should not automatically disqualify a patient from receiving MCS or participating in related clinical trials.

articles.title: Is ultrasound superior to plain radiography for detecting radiolucent soft tissue foreign bodies in the extremities?
articles.takeaway: When suspecting radiolucent soft tissue foreign bodies in an extremity, ultrasound is clearly the modality of choice over plain film. Remember that while this evidence points strongly toward US superiority, these findings are based on limited, small studies, so maintain a high index of suspicion and consider it when initial imaging is negative.
articles.shortSummary: This systematic review compared the utility of ultrasound versus plain radiography for identifying radiolucent soft tissue foreign bodies in the extremities.
articles.summary: This systematic review compared the utility of ultrasound versus plain radiography for identifying radiolucent soft tissue foreign bodies in the extremities. The authors synthesized data from six relevant studies, concluding that ultrasound demonstrated a high sensitivity range of 90% to 99% for detecting these types of objects. In contrast, plain radiography failed to detect any such foreign bodies across the comparative analyses reviewed. While the evidence strongly suggests ultrasound is superior to standard X-ray imaging in this specific scenario, the authors caution that the current literature base suffers from limitations, primarily consisting of small, single-center designs and reliance on experienced operators.

articles.title: Transesophageal Echocardiography for Pulse Evaluation during Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation
articles.takeaway: For immediate bedside decision-making during arrest, TEE appears to expedite the pulse evaluation process compared to manual palpation by offering continuous visualization. However, do not change your standard CPR algorithm based on this; remember that any reported improvements in ROSC are preliminary and need validation from larger randomized studies before altering established protocols.
articles.shortSummary: This recent work compared the utility of Transesophageal Echocardiography (TEE) versus traditional manual palpation for assessing pulses during cardiopulmonary resuscitation.
articles.summary: This recent work compared the utility of Transesophageal Echocardiography (TEE) versus traditional manual palpation for assessing pulses during cardiopulmonary resuscitation. The key finding highlighted that using TEE was associated with significantly shorter decision-making times related to pulse assessment when compared directly to manual methods. This is attributed to TEE's ability to provide rapid and continuous visualization of cardiac structures, streamlining the process of determining the next steps in arrest management. While this suggests a potential benefit in optimizing the time spent making decisions during CPR, the authors are careful to note that any observed differences in major outcomes, such as return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC), are purely exploratory. They strongly emphasize that definitive conclusions regarding clinical impact require confirmation through rigorous randomized, multicenter trials.

articles.title: POCUS literature primer: key papers on first-trimester pregnancy and scrotal POCUS
articles.takeaway: When evaluating early pregnancy or acute scrotal pain, remember that established literature supports using focused ultrasound for rapid triage; specifically, it aids in confirming intrauterine location versus ruling out torsion. While this resource is a helpful educational framework, always interpret the findings within the context of local protocols and clinical suspicion, as these guidelines are meant to support, not replace, hands-on expertise.
articles.shortSummary: This consensus review curates essential literature regarding the use of point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) in two distinct but critical emergency medicine scenarios: first-trimester pregnancy assessment and scrotal pathol…
articles.summary: This consensus review curates essential literature regarding the use of point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) in two distinct but critical emergency medicine scenarios: first-trimester pregnancy assessment and scrotal pathology evaluation. For early gestation, the selected papers reinforce POCUS's utility in differentiating an intrauterine pregnancy from a potentially life-threatening ectopic pregnancy, which is crucial for immediate management decisions. Similarly, in the setting of acute scrotal pain, these foundational articles emphasize the role of ultrasound in rapidly identifying testicular torsion, thereby guiding urgent surgical consultation. Overall, this resource compiles key evidence to help clinicians and trainees integrate POCUS more confidently into their practice pathways.

articles.title: Development of a Predictive Model for Identifying High-Risk Older Adults for Geriatric Emergency Department Screening
articles.takeaway: Consider implementing the GED-SS score at your local ED triage if you are looking to refine risk assessment in older adults compared to existing tools. Since it is designed for automatic calculation using structured data, integrating it into your current workflow should be relatively straightforward. Remember that while its performance metrics look promising, this model's utility must be weighed against the established clinical judgment of the team.
articles.shortSummary: This paper introduces the Geriatric Emergency Department Screening Score (GED-SS), a newly developed tool intended to stratify older adult emergency department patients based on their risk of short-term mortality and ov…
articles.summary: This paper introduces the Geriatric Emergency Department Screening Score (GED-SS), a newly developed tool intended to stratify older adult emergency department patients based on their risk of short-term mortality and overall acute care utilization. The authors report that this structured data model was prospectively validated and performs comparably to, or even better than, established tools like the ISAR score, achieving an AUC of 0.73 in their analysis. The key strength highlighted is its design for automatic calculation using readily available structured data at triage. While it offers a systematic approach to risk stratification that could improve care coordination, readers should note that this represents a model development and validation effort.

articles.title: Ambulance offload delays and patient outcomes: a systematic review
articles.takeaway: While these findings confirm that offloading delays significantly prolong ED stays, don't over-interpret the lack of evidence for mortality; the study itself notes this is due to methodological limitations. Focus on recognizing the operational impact—these delays are a known source of system strain. For bedside practice, remember that while improving throughput is key, current data doesn't definitively prove that minor offload delays cause acute patient harm.
articles.shortSummary: This systematic review synthesized data from four observational studies examining the link between ambulance offload delays (ramping) and various patient outcomes.
articles.summary: This systematic review synthesized data from four observational studies examining the link between ambulance offload delays (ramping) and various patient outcomes. The authors found a consistent association between these delays and prolonged emergency department length of stay, which highlights a significant operational strain on current systems. While one study noted a modest increase in 30-day repeat ambulance attendance following delays, the evidence linking offload delay directly to increased patient mortality or acute clinical deterioration remains inconclusive across the included literature. The authors caution that methodological weaknesses are pervasive, citing varied definitions of delay and limited control for baseline illness severity, which complicates drawing firm causal conclusions.

articles.title: Point-of-care lung ultrasound during atropine titration in severe organophosphate poisoning: A case report
articles.takeaway: While this is a single case report, the correlation shown between decreasing B-lines on LUS and improving clinical status during atropine titration warrants consideration. Consider using repeat lung ultrasound as a non-invasive adjunct to monitor for pulmonary resolution or deterioration when managing severe cholinergic toxidrome. Remember that this finding is observational, so do not rely solely on LUS findings to guide life-saving resuscitation decisions.
articles.shortSummary: This case report explores the utility of serial point-of-care lung ultrasound (LUS) as an adjunct tool while managing severe organophosphate poisoning, particularly during the critical phase of atropine titration.
articles.summary: This case report explores the utility of serial point-of-care lung ultrasound (LUS) as an adjunct tool while managing severe organophosphate poisoning, particularly during the critical phase of atropine titration. The authors describe a patient presenting with a profound cholinergic toxidrome and subsequent hypoxic respiratory distress. They utilized repeat LUS measurements to monitor pulmonary status alongside escalating doses of atropine. Notably, they observed a progressive decrease in B-line burden that correlated temporally with both the cumulative atropine administration and the patient's overall clinical improvement. This suggests that serial ultrasound assessment might offer an objective way to track pulmonary changes during toxicologic resuscitation efforts where endpoints are often subjective.

articles.title: Guideline-Integrated Large Language Models Improve Decision Support for Acute Ear, Nose and Throat Emergencies
articles.takeaway: When considering using LLMs for complex otolaryngology decision support in the ED, remember that RAG integration appears crucial for ensuring outputs align with current guidelines. This suggests a more reliable tool than raw LLM output alone. However, treat these suggestions as supplementary guidance; always confirm critical management steps against established institutional protocols.
articles.shortSummary: This piece explores how integrating Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) into Large Language Models (LLMs) can significantly boost the reliability and adherence to established guidelines when using AI for decision suppo…
articles.summary: This piece explores how integrating Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) into Large Language Models (LLMs) can significantly boost the reliability and adherence to established guidelines when using AI for decision support in acute otolaryngology emergencies. The core finding is that these RAG-enabled models outperform baseline LLM performance by grounding their outputs in specific, current clinical guidelines. This suggests a promising avenue for improving evidence-based care pathways for ED presentations like sudden sensorineural hearing loss or epistaxis. Overall, the authors argue that this integration enhances both the safety and accuracy of AI-generated management plans.

articles.title: A Woman With Rapidly Progressive Weakness
articles.takeaway: When encountering rapidly progressive flaccid paralysis with a distinct sensory level in a patient with CKD, immediately consider the full spectrum beyond simple peripheral neuropathy; think about transverse myelitis or advanced toxin-induced neuropathies. The combination of lower extremity weakness and specific sensory loss warrants urgent electrodiagnostic testing to characterize the pattern of injury.
articles.shortSummary: This case report details a presentation of rapidly progressive, nontraumatic lower extremity weakness in a patient with chronic kidney disease.
articles.summary: This case report details a presentation of rapidly progressive, nontraumatic lower extremity weakness in a patient with chronic kidney disease. The key findings included flaccid paralysis in the legs, absent reflexes, and a specific pattern of dissociative sensory loss affecting pain and temperature below the nipple-to-xiphoid level. Given the acute onset and profound motor deficit without clear trauma, the differential diagnosis is broad, but the combination of CKD and this specific neurological picture warrants careful consideration of underlying neuromuscular or metabolic etiologies. The clinical presentation strongly suggests an acute polyneuropathy or myelopathy process that requires urgent workup.

articles.title: EM@3AM: Meningococcal Disease
articles.takeaway: When encountering a patient with fever, headache, lethargy, nuchal rigidity, and petechiae, always maintain a high index of suspicion for meningococcemia until proven otherwise. Expedite CSF analysis and initiate appropriate empirical antibiotics immediately after obtaining necessary cultures, even if initial workup is negative or pending. Remember that early recognition drastically improves outcomes.
articles.shortSummary: This emDocs review uses a clinical case presentation to reinforce key aspects of suspected meningococcal disease management in the emergency department.
articles.summary: This emDocs review uses a clinical case presentation to reinforce key aspects of suspected meningococcal disease management in the emergency department. The scenario describes an adolescent presenting with classic signs of meningitis, including fever, headache, lethargy, nuchal rigidity, and a petechial rash, alongside mild hemodynamic instability. Given that Neisseria meningitidis is a significant cause of bacterial meningitis associated with high morbidity and mortality, prompt recognition and appropriate workup are paramount in the ED setting. The review emphasizes the constellation of symptoms—especially the combination of altered mental status, signs of meningeal irritation, and rash—that warrants immediate consideration for this diagnosis. Understanding these clinical hallmarks helps guide timely diagnostic testing and initiation of empiric therapy.

editions.label: 7 June 2026
editions.headline: POCUS Guidance and Antibiotic Reassessment: Key Edits for Today's ED
editions.summary:
The evidence base continues to refine procedural best practices, starting with pediatric care. For routine urethral catheterization in children, integrating real-time Point-of-Care Ultrasound (POCUS) appears highly valuable; systematic reviews suggest it significantly boosts first-attempt success rates while minimizing unnecessary attempts.

In infectious workups, the message remains clear: do not default to antibiotics for acute wheezing. A major trial definitively showed that azithromycin offered no benefit in preschool wheezing, suggesting we must be much more judicious with empirical coverage when colonization is found.

For complex vascular emergencies, management strategies are shifting toward targeted approaches. In PE care, consider ultrasound-guided catheter-directed fibrinolysis as a localized alternative when systemic thrombolysis carries prohibitive bleeding risks. Furthermore, in trauma, while endovascular repair shows promise for axillosubclavian injuries compared to open methods, the reliance on retrospective data means caution is warranted before changing standard protocols.

These reads underscore a theme of precision: whether it's guiding a simple procedure with ultrasound or withholding antibiotics based on clear trial data, today’s literature demands that we move beyond generalized care toward highly specific, evidence-backed interventions.
editions.notes: Remember to always temper exciting procedural findings with the limitations of the underlying study design.

articles.title: Point-of-Care Ultrasound for Pediatric Urethral Catheterization: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of Randomized Controlled Trials
articles.takeaway: For routine pediatric urethral catheterization, incorporating real-time POCUS appears to be a valuable addition that should improve first-attempt success and reduce unnecessary attempts. This approach seems safe enough for integration without compromising ED throughput, but remember this evidence is based on RCTs, so always assess the local workflow feasibility when implementing it.
articles.shortSummary: This systematic review and meta-analysis synthesized data from randomized controlled trials to evaluate the role of real-time Point-of-Care Ultrasound (POCUS) during urethral catheterization in pediatric patients.
articles.summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis synthesized data from randomized controlled trials to evaluate the role of real-time Point-of-Care Ultrasound (POCUS) during urethral catheterization in pediatric patients. The authors concluded that utilizing ultrasound guidance significantly boosts the success rate on the first attempt while markedly decreasing the number of futile attempts, which is a major clinical concern in this population. Beyond procedural metrics, the review highlighted secondary benefits, noting improvements in caregiver satisfaction and reduced patient distress without negatively impacting the overall efficiency or workflow within the emergency department setting. Given these highly actionable findings, the authors strongly support integrating ultrasound guidance into standard pediatric emergency care protocols.

articles.title: AZ-SWED Trial: Azithromycin Does Not Improve Preschool Wheezing Outcomes
articles.takeaway: Given the lack of benefit shown by azithromycin in the AZ-SWED trial, withhold empirical antibiotics for acute preschool wheezing unless there is a strong suspicion for a specific bacterial etiology. Continue standard supportive care including bronchodilators and corticosteroids as indicated, but do not rely on antibiotic coverage based solely on positive nasal swabs.
articles.shortSummary: The AZ-SWED trial provides a clear randomized evidence base regarding the role of azithromycin in managing preschool wheezing, which is a very common presentation in the emergency department setting.
articles.summary: The AZ-SWED trial provides a clear randomized evidence base regarding the role of azithromycin in managing preschool wheezing, which is a very common presentation in the emergency department setting. The study randomized children with wheezing to receive either azithromycin or placebo and ultimately found no discernible clinical benefit from the antibiotic intervention, irrespective of whether nasopharyngeal bacteria were detected during workup. This suggests that the underlying pathophysiology driving acute wheezing episodes in this age group is likely not primarily bacterial, even when colonization is present. The authors emphasize that routine use of antibiotics for presumed viral or atypical causes of wheezing should be reconsidered based on these findings.

articles.title: Tenecteplase Unseats Low-Dose Alteplase Favored for Asian Patients
articles.takeaway: For bleeding-prone patients needing thrombolysis prior to thrombectomy, standard tenecteplase appears to be the preferred agent over low-dose alteplase based on this Japanese data. This suggests a potential shift toward using TNKase as the bridging therapy in these high-risk scenarios. However, given that this is from a single registry/trial, exercise caution and consider local guidelines when making changes at the bedside.
articles.shortSummary: A recent Japanese trial provides compelling data suggesting that standard-dose tenecteplase (TNKase) may be superior to low-dose alteplase when used as a bridging agent for patients undergoing mechanical thrombectomy, p…
articles.summary: A recent Japanese trial provides compelling data suggesting that standard-dose tenecteplase (TNKase) may be superior to low-dose alteplase when used as a bridging agent for patients undergoing mechanical thrombectomy, particularly those with elevated bleeding risk. The study's primary finding indicates significantly better early reperfusion outcomes in the group receiving TNKase compared to the low-dose alteplase cohort. This suggests that standard tenecteplase might offer an advantage over lower doses of recombinant tissue plasminogen activator (alteplase) in this specific, high-risk subset of stroke patients undergoing endovascular therapy. While the data is encouraging for optimizing reperfusion strategies, it's important to remember this finding pertains to a bridging role in thrombectomy.

articles.title: Catheter-Directed Fibrinolysis for Pulmonary Embolism: Is It Old NEWS?
articles.takeaway: For acute PE management, consider ultrasound-guided catheter-directed fibrinolysis when systemic thrombolysis is high risk or impractical. This approach offers a more localized means to break down clot burden, potentially sparing the patient from systemic bleeding risks associated with full reperfusion therapy. Remember that procedural expertise and careful selection of patients are key considerations here.
articles.shortSummary: This article details the use of ultrasound-guided, catheter-directed fibrinolysis as a therapeutic option for acute pulmonary embolism (PE).
articles.summary: This article details the use of ultrasound-guided, catheter-directed fibrinolysis as a therapeutic option for acute pulmonary embolism (PE). The authors present data on this minimally invasive approach, which aims to directly administer thrombolytic agents into the clot burden within the pulmonary vasculature. This technique is particularly relevant for patients where systemic thrombolysis or embolectomy might be contraindicated or difficult to perform due to underlying comorbidities or technical challenges. It represents a more targeted intervention compared to standard reperfusion strategies. The discussion highlights the procedural nuances and potential benefits of localizing fibrinolytic activity directly at the site of embolus.

Fundamentally, the guidelines reinforce core therapies, specifically highlighting red blood cells and tranexamic acid as essential components for initial management. Furthermore, they provide practical guidance on product logistics, noting that while standard care is established, freeze-dried plasma and whole blood can offer distinct advantages when operating in more remote or austere settings. These consensus statements aim to standardize care while remaining adaptable to diverse geographical challenges.

This distinction is important because it suggests that while the overall survival picture might look similar at 30 days, the underlying risk factors driving mortality are distinct depending on the initial cardiac arrest event.

articles.title: Is ultrasound superior to plain radiography for detecting radiolucent soft tissue foreign bodies in the extremities?
articles.takeaway: When you suspect a radiolucent soft tissue foreign body in an extremity, prioritize obtaining an ultrasound over plain films; it has significantly higher sensitivity for detection. Remember that this recommendation is based on limited literature, so maintain clinical suspicion even if the initial ultrasound is negative. Don't rely solely on this finding to rule out all foreign bodies.
articles.shortSummary: This systematic review compared the utility of ultrasound versus plain radiography for identifying radiolucent soft tissue foreign bodies in the extremities.
articles.summary: This systematic review compared the utility of ultrasound versus plain radiography for identifying radiolucent soft tissue foreign bodies in the extremities. The authors synthesized data from six relevant studies, finding a marked difference in diagnostic yield between the two modalities. Specifically, ultrasound demonstrated a high sensitivity range of 90% to 99% for detecting these types of objects, whereas plain radiographs failed to detect any foreign bodies in the comparative analyses reviewed. While the evidence strongly suggests ultrasound is superior for this specific task, the authors caution that the current literature pool suffers from significant limitations, primarily consisting of small sample sizes and single-center designs. Therefore, while the trend points toward ultrasound being the preferred tool, larger prospective studies are necessary before definitive guidelines can be established.

While the literature supports its use, this compilation serves as a starting point; always correlate findings with clinical context and be mindful of institutional protocols when interpreting these images.

For early pregnancy care, the literature emphasizes using ultrasound to confirm intrauterine gestation while helping rule out ectopic pregnancies. Similarly, in the setting of acute scrotal pain, the reviewed material reinforces the utility of POCUS for timely recognition of testicular torsion. Overall, this resource aims to support evidence-based implementation across both domains.

Remember that while its performance is promising compared to tools like ISAR, it remains a screening adjunct and does not replace comprehensive clinical judgment.

articles.summary: This paper introduces the Geriatric Emergency Department Screening Score (GED-SS), a novel predictive tool designed specifically for older adults presenting to the emergency department. The goal was to create a structured, easily calculable model that can help stratify risk for short-term mortality and overall acute care utilization in this vulnerable population. Validation showed that the GED-SS performs comparably to or even better than established tools like the ISAR score, achieving an AUC of 0.73. The key strength highlighted is its structured data format, suggesting it can be integrated into triage systems for automated risk assessment. This offers a tangible improvement in our ability to identify those who need more intensive follow-up.

articles.title: Ambulance offload delays and patient outcomes: a systematic review
articles.takeaway: While these findings confirm that ambulance ramping significantly prolongs ED stays, do not over-interpret this as definitive proof of direct patient harm. Clinicians should remain aware of the operational impact on throughput, but remember that current evidence is methodologically limited regarding mortality or acute deterioration. Future policy changes must wait for studies employing more rigorous causal inference methods.
articles.shortSummary: This systematic review synthesized data from four observational studies examining the link between ambulance offload delays (ramping) and various patient outcomes in emergency departments.
articles.summary: This systematic review synthesized data from four observational studies examining the link between ambulance offload delays (ramping) and various patient outcomes in emergency departments. The authors found a consistent association between these delays and prolonged overall ED length of stay, which highlights a significant operational burden on strained systems. While one study noted a modest increase in 30-day repeat ambulance attendance following delay, the evidence linking offload delays directly to increased patient mortality or acute clinical deterioration remains weak. This uncertainty is largely attributed to methodological limitations across the included studies, including varied definitions of 'delay' and limited control for baseline illness risk.

The core finding is that coupling LLMs with structured, reliable knowledge bases significantly boosts the model's ability to provide accurate and guideline-adherent recommendations compared to using the raw LLM alone. For emergency physicians managing complex presentations like sudden sensorineural hearing loss or epistaxis, this suggests a pathway toward more robust, evidence-backed digital assistance. The authors argue that RAG integration improves not just diagnostic accuracy but also overall management planning and adherence to established protocols in the ED setting.

Examination revealed profound flaccid paralysis in the legs, absent reflexes, and a distinct pattern of sensory loss affecting pain and temperature below the nipple-to-xiphoid level. The clinical picture is highly suggestive of an acute neuromuscular process requiring urgent workup given her underlying comorbidities. While the specific etiology isn't detailed here, the presentation strongly mimics several critical diagnoses that can present with acute polyneuropathy or motor weakness in the emergency setting.

editions.label: 27 May 2026
editions.headline: Sharp Tools, Higher Thresholds
editions.summary:
Today's set rewards clinicians who like useful tools but distrust easy hype. The cleanest bedside wins are practical: serratus anterior plane block looks increasingly ready for rib-fracture analgesia in the ED, trained emergency physicians can diagnose DVT accurately and much faster with compression ultrasound, and ultrasound again outperforms plain films for radiolucent foreign bodies.

The pulmonary embolism cluster is where the editorial tension sits. HI-PEITHO suggests a narrower benefit for catheter-directed fibrinolysis in selected intermediate-risk PE, while the paired commentary and First10EM critique both argue for a much higher threshold before turning procedural capability into routine care. That same caution threads through other papers: postoperative transfusion thresholds still need discipline, imported head-injury pathways may save resources but not automatically safely, and presepsin remains an adjunct rather than a biomarker escape hatch from clinical judgment.

There are also a few quieter but sticky practice points worth carrying onto shift: TWIST plus POCUS helps in paediatric torsion but should not be used to rule it out, sepsis-induced new AF still does not justify reflex anticoagulation, and preceding OHCA alone should not exclude a patient from mechanical circulatory support conversations. Overall, this is a reading set about using sharper tools with a higher threshold, not about chasing every shiny intervention.
editions.notes: Best immediate reads: SAPB for rib fractures, bedside DVT ultrasound, the HI-PEITHO trial/critique pair, and the CAP guideline update.

That matters, because staffing debates are often framed with more certainty than the literature can support.

This is a nuanced escalation paper, not a signal to catheterise every stable submassive PE.

articles.title: 2025 guideline updates for community-acquired pneumonia diagnosis and management
articles.takeaway: Expect more justification for ultrasound-first thinking and shorter antibiotic courses in uncomplicated CAP. Treat the patient’s risk profile, not every infiltrate with the same reflexive antibiotic script.

Lung ultrasound gets more legitimacy, some viral-positive low-risk patients may not need antibiotics, and shorter treatment courses are increasingly supported in stable non-severe disease. articles.title: TOP: Liberal or restrictive post-op transfusion
articles.takeaway: Do not let cardiac comorbidity automatically drive you to liberal transfusion. Individualise for ischemia and bleeding risk, but restrictive practice still looks like the default position to defend.

articles.takeaway: Read this alongside HI-PEITHO, not instead of it. The main practical value is sharpening thresholds for escalation and avoiding the slide from promising procedure to overused procedure.

articles.takeaway: Use TWIST and POCUS to structure urgency, not to dismiss a child with persistent torsion concern. High clinical suspicion still beats a reassuring score when the story or exam feels wrong.

editions.label: 26 May 2026
editions.headline: Evidence With Restraint: Targeted Gains Without the Hype
editions.summary:
Today's set is less about sweeping disruption and more about tightening judgment. Trauma transfusion, catheter-directed PE therapy, regional anaesthesia for rib fractures, bedside DVT ultrasound and paediatric IV access all point toward targeted interventions that can help when used in the right patients and systems—but several papers also warn against reading operational or procedural enthusiasm as proof of universal benefit.

The strongest practical signals are the SAPB trial for rib-fracture analgesia, bedside compression ultrasound for DVT, and better paediatric cannulation with a vein locator. By contrast, the PE literature is a good lesson in restraint: HI-PEITHO raises the possibility of benefit in a narrow subgroup, while contemporaneous commentary reminds us that invasive escalation still needs a high threshold and patient-centred endpoints.

Across sepsis, pneumonia, torsion, anticoagulation and biomarkers, the recurring theme is the same: use tools to refine care, not to outsource judgment. The win is not more technology at any cost; it is better selection, faster decisions and fewer automatic habits.
editions.notes: Strongest bedside reads: SAPB for rib fractures, EP-performed DVT ultrasound, paediatric vein-locator data, and the paired pro/con PE commentary around HI-PEITHO.

For a common ED pain point, that is exactly the sort of modest technology win worth noticing. It will not replace skill, but it may reduce distress and procedural drag in the right population.

articles.title: Serratus Anterior Plane Block for Acute Rib Fractures in the Emergency Department: A Randomized Controlled Trial

editions.label: 25 May 2026
editions.headline: Trauma Resuscitation & Targeted Interventions: What's Changing at the Bedside
editions.summary:
The evidence base continues to refine our approach to high-acuity resuscitation. In trauma, the TOWAR data offers a compelling argument that whole, type O blood en route may be a viable, perhaps simpler, alternative to full component therapy when managing hemorrhagic shock. Meanwhile, for pulmonary embolism, the literature is pushing toward more targeted lysis; the HI-PEITHO trial suggests that adding ultrasound-guided, catheter-directed fibrinolysis to standard anticoagulation may specifically benefit the sickest intermediate-risk patients by mitigating short-term hemodynamic dips.

Beyond massive transfusion, we see refinement in procedural care. For routine vascular access in pediatrics, the use of liquid crystal vein locators appears to offer a tangible benefit in first-attempt success rates, potentially reducing both provider time and patient distress. Similarly, for acute rib fractures, the data supports integrating the Serratus Anterior Plane Block as a safe, effective adjunct to standard analgesia, offering a non-opioid edge.

These findings—from optimizing blood products in trauma to refining regional anesthesia for simple fractures—underscore a trend toward highly specific, evidence-backed interventions. Today’s reading set demands that we move beyond generalized protocols and critically assess which advanced tools or procedural tweaks offer the most measurable, actionable benefit for our most common, yet most complex, presentations.
editions.notes: Keep an eye on the interplay between advanced imaging, targeted therapies, and established resuscitation principles.

articles.title: Association between patient-to-nurse ratios in emergency departments and patient, staff, and organizational outcomes: a systematic review
articles.takeaway: While the concept of setting nurse ratios sounds appealing for quality improvement, remember that this systematic review's key findings on ED mortality are not available in the provided text. Clinicians should approach the general literature suggesting ratio benefits with caution, recognizing that the ED environment may confound these associations. For now, the evidence synthesis is incomplete, so major changes based solely on this overview are premature.
articles.shortSummary: This systematic review synthesized the existing literature to determine if establishing specific patient-to-nurse ratios in the emergency department (ED) is associated with improved patient mortality.
articles.summary: This systematic review synthesized the existing literature to determine if establishing specific patient-to-nurse ratios in the emergency department (ED) is associated with improved patient mortality. While the general literature suggests that setting nurse ratios benefits both patients and staff in various care settings, the applicability of these findings specifically to the chaotic environment of the ED remains uncertain. The authors conducted a comprehensive search across major databases for studies published between 2004 and 2025, employing two independent reviewers for data extraction. The primary goal was to quantify the relationship between these staffing ratios and overall ED mortality. However, the provided excerpt only details the methodology, noting the search strategy and the rigorous review process, without presenting the actual findings regarding mortality.

articles.title: Whole Blood Not Better Than Components En Route to Hospital After Traumatic Injury
articles.takeaway: When managing a trauma patient at risk of hemorrhagic shock, administering whole, type O blood en route appears to be as effective as component therapy based on the TOWAR data. This suggests that whole blood can be a viable, potentially simpler, resuscitation choice at the point of care. However, always consider the local protocol and the specific clinical context, as this finding compares outcomes, not necessarily the optimal resuscitation strategy for every patient.

articles.summary: The TOWAR randomized trial provides data suggesting that for trauma patients anticipated to develop hemorrhagic shock, administering whole, type O blood en route to the hospital yields outcomes comparable to administering pre-transfusion blood components. This finding challenges the historical emphasis on component therapy for massive transfusion protocols in the acute trauma setting. The study's primary conclusion is that whole blood can serve as an acceptable alternative to component-based resuscitation in this high-risk population. This is a notable piece of evidence for guiding immediate pre-hospital or point-of-care transfusion decisions in trauma.

articles.title: HI-PEITHO Trial: Ultrasound-facilitated, Catheter Directed Fibrinolysis for PE
articles.takeaway: For intermediate-risk PE, standard anticoagulation remains the essential initial step. Consider US-CDT plus anticoagulation primarily for those patients exhibiting signs of significant hemodynamic instability, as this combination showed benefit in preventing short-term decompensation in the sickest group. Remember that this advanced therapy is not indicated for all intermediate-risk patients and requires careful risk stratification.
articles.shortSummary: The HI-PEITHO trial was a randomized controlled trial designed to test whether adding ultrasound-facilitated, catheter-directed fibrinolysis (US-CDT) to standard anticoagulation improves outcomes in intermediate-risk pu…
articles.summary: The HI-PEITHO trial was a randomized controlled trial designed to test whether adding ultrasound-facilitated, catheter-directed fibrinolysis (US-CDT) to standard anticoagulation improves outcomes in intermediate-risk pulmonary embolism (PE) patients. The primary question addressed was whether this combination therapy offers an advantage over anticoagulation alone in this specific cohort. The key takeaway from the analysis is that the addition of US-CDT appears to confer a benefit specifically in mitigating short-term hemodynamic decompensation among the sickest subset of intermediate-risk PE patients. Overall, the trial suggests a role for this advanced intervention in a carefully selected, high-risk subset, while maintaining anticoagulation as the cornerstone of management.

articles.title: Effect of a liquid crystal vein locator on intravenous catheterization in young children: a randomized trial
articles.takeaway: For routine PIVC in young children, incorporating a liquid crystal vein locator appears beneficial, as it correlates with higher first-attempt success and reduced procedural time. This could translate to less distress for the child and faster access for the provider. However, remember this is based on a randomized trial, and its utility should be weighed against the added equipment and time required for setup at the bedside.
articles.shortSummary: This randomized trial assessed the utility of a liquid crystal vein locator during peripheral intravenous catheterization (PIVC) specifically in the pediatric population.
articles.summary: This randomized trial assessed the utility of a liquid crystal vein locator during peripheral intravenous catheterization (PIVC) specifically in the pediatric population. The primary finding is that employing this locator significantly improved procedural metrics compared to standard techniques. Specifically, the group using the locator demonstrated a statistically significant increase in first-attempt success rates, required fewer total puncture attempts, and achieved shorter overall procedure times. These results suggest a tangible benefit to the efficiency and potentially the experience of the child during necessary vascular access in the emergency setting. Overall, the data points toward a practical advantage for this technology in pediatric emergency medicine.

articles.title: Serratus Anterior Plane Block for Acute Rib Fractures in the Emergency Department: A Randomized Controlled Trial
articles.takeaway: Given the significant improvement in PIC scores, consider adding SAPB to your standard analgesic regimen for acute rib fractures in the ED. Since the procedure was reported as safe and feasible for ED personnel, this offers a valuable, non-opioid adjunct. Remember that while promising, this is based on a single trial, so integrate it thoughtfully into your local protocols.
articles.shortSummary: This randomized controlled trial evaluated the utility of adding a Serratus Anterior Plane Block (SAPB) to standard analgesic management for acute rib fractures encountered in the emergency department.
articles.summary: This randomized controlled trial evaluated the utility of adding a Serratus Anterior Plane Block (SAPB) to standard analgesic management for acute rib fractures encountered in the emergency department. The primary finding was that incorporating the SAPB significantly improved Pain Intensity Control (PIC) scores among this patient cohort. The authors emphasize that the procedure was performed safely by emergency physicians, suggesting it is a practical and scalable adjunct therapy. Overall, the data supports the integration of this regional anesthetic technique into the routine ED armamentarium for managing rib fracture pain.

articles.title: 2025 guideline updates for community-acquired pneumonia diagnosis and management
articles.takeaway: When managing nonsevere CAP, remember to consider de-escalating antibiotic therapy to a minimum of three days for stable patients, which is a key update. Also, don't overlook the utility of lung ultrasound when you have an experienced reader available, as this is gaining formal support. However, be mindful that these guidelines are updated, and local institutional protocols should always be cross-referenced, especially regarding viral testing and antibiotic necessity.
articles.shortSummary: The new 2025 American Thoracic Society guidelines provide an updated framework for diagnosing and managing community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) encountered in the emergency department.
articles.summary: The new 2025 American Thoracic Society guidelines provide an updated framework for diagnosing and managing community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) encountered in the emergency department. These guidelines touch upon imaging modalities, antibiotic stewardship, and the role of corticosteroids. Notably, the recommendations appear to bolster the utility of lung ultrasound when performed by experienced clinicians, suggesting it's a valuable tool in the ED setting. Furthermore, there's a shift in antibiotic management, suggesting that for otherwise healthy patients who test positive for a virus, antibiotics may not be necessary. Clinically, the guidelines advocate for shortening the duration of antibiotic therapy, recommending a minimum course of three days for both stable outpatients and admitted patients with nonsevere CAP.

articles.title: TOP: Liberal or restrictive post-op transfusion
articles.takeaway: For high-risk patients post-major surgery, the data from TOP generally support a more restrictive approach to transfusion, suggesting that aiming for a hemoglobin target of 7 g/dL or higher may be safer than liberal transfusion practices. However, remember that the decision must be individualized, and the risk of bleeding versus the risk of transfusion-related complications needs careful weighing at the bedside.
articles.shortSummary: The TOP trial addressed a common dilemma in high-risk surgical patients: whether to adopt a liberal or restrictive approach to managing postoperative anemia.
articles.summary: The TOP trial addressed a common dilemma in high-risk surgical patients: whether to adopt a liberal or restrictive approach to managing postoperative anemia. Specifically, it compared a liberal transfusion threshold, defined as maintaining hemoglobin levels below 10 g/dL, against a more restrictive threshold of below 7 g/dL in adults with known cardiac risk following major surgery. The primary endpoint was the reduction of 90-day all-cause death or major ischemic events. While the overall trend in the literature suggests favoring more conservative transfusion practices, this trial provides a direct comparison in a vulnerable cohort, which is valuable for refining institutional protocols.

articles.title: Catheter-Directed Fibrinolysis for Pulmonary Embolism: Is It Old NEWS?
articles.takeaway: For acute PE management, consider ultrasound-guided, catheter-directed fibrinolysis as a targeted alternative to systemic thrombolytics, especially in patients with high bleeding risk. While the technique appears promising for localized clot lysis, remember that its utility should be weighed against the risks of procedural complications and the need for careful hemodynamic monitoring. Always review the institutional guidelines regarding appropriate patient selection for this advanced intervention.
articles.shortSummary: This article details the use of ultrasound-guided, catheter-directed fibrinolysis as a treatment strategy for acute pulmonary embolism.
articles.summary: This article details the use of ultrasound-guided, catheter-directed fibrinolysis as a treatment strategy for acute pulmonary embolism. The authors present data on this minimally invasive approach, which aims to directly administer thrombolytic agents to the pulmonary embolus site. The technique utilizes ultrasound guidance to improve visualization and placement accuracy of the catheter within the pulmonary vasculature. This represents a more targeted intervention compared to systemic thrombolysis, which carries a higher risk of bleeding complications. The findings suggest this method offers a potentially safer and more localized means of achieving clot lysis in PE patients.

articles.title: Modified brain injury guidelines in a regional Australian setting indicate significant resource savings but also safety concerns
articles.takeaway: When considering adopting TBI stratification guidelines like mBIG, remember that while resource savings are appealing, the evidence base is foreign to your local practice. Do not implement these pathways based solely on US data; instead, critically evaluate the local resource utilization patterns and the specific criteria for escalation to ensure safety is maintained. Be cautious about making sweeping changes without local validation.
articles.shortSummary: Traumatic brain injury remains a significant burden in the emergency department, with resource utilization—including unnecessary neurosurgical consultations and repeat CTs—being a major concern even though most injuries…
articles.summary: Traumatic brain injury remains a significant burden in the emergency department, with resource utilization—including unnecessary neurosurgical consultations and repeat CTs—being a major concern even though most injuries are mild. The modified brain injury guidelines (mBIG), which originated in the US, provide a structured approach to stratifying TBI severity to safely reduce resource use. While these US-developed pathways have shown promise in improving resource efficiency without compromising patient outcomes, their applicability and safety profile within the Australian clinical context remain unvalidated. This local assessment is crucial because current regional guidelines, such as those in NSW, may lead to high utilization of resources like neurosurgical consultations for routine findings. Therefore, understanding the local impact of adopting such modified guidelines is important for optimizing care.

articles.title: Evaluating the TWIST score and point-of-care ultrasound for paediatric testicular torsion
articles.takeaway: When evaluating acute scrotal pain in a child, remember that while the TWIST score and POCUS are highly accurate tools for risk stratification, the residual false-negative rate means you cannot rely on a negative score to completely exclude torsion. If the clinical suspicion remains high despite a low score, proceed with caution and consider further workup or urology consultation. The objective ultrasound findings provide strong supporting data when interpreting the score.
articles.shortSummary: This study assessed the diagnostic utility of the TWIST score combined with point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) for testicular torsion in children up to 15 years old, specifically when interpreted by emergency physicians o…
articles.summary: This study assessed the diagnostic utility of the TWIST score combined with point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) for testicular torsion in children up to 15 years old, specifically when interpreted by emergency physicians or pediatricians. The findings indicate that the TWIST score maintains high sensitivity (91%) and specificity (95%) even when performed by non-urology specialists in the emergency department setting. While the overall diagnostic accuracy is high, the authors caution that the non-negligible false-negative rate means the score should not be used to definitively rule out torsion. Furthermore, when defining positivity based on objective ultrasound findings like impaired blood flow or the whirlpool sign, the sensitivity and specificity remain robust, though the positive predictive value is lower.

articles.title: Journal update monthly top five
articles.takeaway: When managing septic shock, remember that while prognostic factors are actively being researched, no single factor provides a definitive cutoff for predicting high mortality. Focus on aggressive, guideline-directed resuscitation while remaining vigilant for subtle clinical deterioration, as the literature suggests risk stratification remains complex. Don't over-rely on any single biomarker or clinical score without considering the patient's overall trajectory.
articles.shortSummary: This month's digest presents five high-yield articles from outside of emergency medicine, curated by South West England residents using a multimodal search approach.
articles.summary: This month's digest presents five high-yield articles from outside of emergency medicine, curated by South West England residents using a multimodal search approach. A standout piece is a systematic review and meta-analysis focusing on prognostic factors for mortality in septic shock. The authors highlight the persistent clinical challenge of identifying patients at high risk of death despite ongoing resuscitation efforts. This review synthesizes data on various factors associated with early mortality in adults with septic shock. While the overall utility is noted, the authors emphasize that a clear, definitive risk factor analysis remains elusive, making this a 'head turner' piece rather than an immediate 'game changer.'

articles.title: Point-of-care ultrasound by trained emergency physicians versus radiologists for deep vein thrombosis diagnosis: a prospective blinded study on diagnostic accuracy and time efficiency
articles.takeaway: Given the high sensitivity and specificity, you can feel confident using trained ED personnel for initial DVT screening via compression ultrasound. The most impactful finding is the massive reduction in time-to-diagnosis, which could significantly improve ED throughput. Remember that while the accuracy is high, these findings suggest a need to evaluate if this efficiency translates into tangible downstream benefits, like reduced length of stay.
articles.shortSummary: This prospective, blinded study compared the diagnostic utility and efficiency of point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) performed by trained emergency physicians against the established standard of care, which was radiologis…
articles.summary: This prospective, blinded study compared the diagnostic utility and efficiency of point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) performed by trained emergency physicians against the established standard of care, which was radiologist-performed Doppler ultrasound, for suspected deep vein thrombosis (DVT). The results were quite compelling, showing that the emergency physicians achieved high diagnostic accuracy, reporting a sensitivity of 97.0% and a specificity of 99.3%. Furthermore, the time savings were dramatic, with the median time-to-diagnosis being one hour for the ED physicians compared to four hours for the radiology team. The excellent interobserver agreement, with kappa values generally above 0.90, suggests that the bedside approach is not only accurate but also significantly streamlines the diagnostic pathway for these patients.

articles.title: Design Refinements for Sepsis Albumin Trials: Building on ICARUS-ED Experience
articles.takeaway: The feasibility demonstrated in ICARUS-ED suggests that incorporating concentrated albumin into early sepsis protocols in the ED is logistically viable, even with mixed patient populations. While the trial confirms the ability to enroll and manage these patients, remember that the high rate of confirmed infection (95%) is impressive but doesn't negate the underlying diagnostic uncertainty of undifferentiated sepsis. Proceed with caution, recognizing this is pilot data informing future, larger trials.
articles.shortSummary: Williams et al.
articles.summary: Williams et al. report on the ICARUS-ED pilot randomized controlled trial, which explored the use of concentrated albumin for undifferentiated sepsis management directly within the emergency department setting. The authors highlight the trial's success in establishing a feasible framework for conducting real-time sepsis trials, which is a significant logistical hurdle in this area. Notably, the study managed to enroll and maintain high protocol compliance, even incorporating ward-based patients alongside those destined for the ICU. Furthermore, they confirmed the presence of infection in a high percentage of the enrolled cohort, which is valuable given the diagnostic ambiguity inherent in early sepsis presentation. This work provides important preliminary data on optimizing resuscitation strategies in the acute ED setting.

articles.title: Naloxone administration associated with improved survival in PEA out-of-hospital cardiac arrests
articles.takeaway: For OHCA patients presenting in PEA, the data suggests a potential benefit to administering naloxone prehospital, as it was associated with improved survival to discharge. However, remember this is observational, so do not change your standard protocol based on this alone. Furthermore, do not anticipate a benefit for patients presenting with shockable rhythms or asystole.
articles.shortSummary: This retrospective cohort study examined the link between prehospital naloxone administration and survival outcomes in patients who experienced out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (OHCA).
articles.summary: This retrospective cohort study examined the link between prehospital naloxone administration and survival outcomes in patients who experienced out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (OHCA). The authors found a specific association: naloxone use correlated with improved survival to hospital discharge only in the subset of OHCA patients presenting in pulseless electrical activity (PEA). Notably, the intervention showed no associated benefit for patients presenting with shockable rhythms or asystole. The conclusion emphasizes that while the data suggests a benefit in PEA, the study design necessitates that future confirmation of true causality must come from prospective, randomized controlled trials.

articles.title: Systematic Review of Pediatric Ketamine in Emergency Department Procedural Sedation: Frequency and Predictors of Adverse Events
articles.takeaway: When considering ketamine for procedural sedation in pediatrics, remember that the literature synthesis is focused on compiling adverse event frequencies and predictors. While this review is helpful for a general overview, the clinical utility of any identified predictor variable should be interpreted cautiously due to the varied methodologies across included studies. Always maintain vigilance for signs of adverse reactions, as the pooled estimates provide a general guide rather than a definitive protocol change.
articles.shortSummary: This systematic review synthesizes the existing literature regarding the use of intravenous ketamine for procedural sedation in the pediatric emergency department.
articles.summary: This systematic review synthesizes the existing literature regarding the use of intravenous ketamine for procedural sedation in the pediatric emergency department. The primary goal was to consolidate the reported rates of various adverse events associated with this anesthetic agent in this specific population. Furthermore, the authors undertook an effort to catalogue and estimate the clinical utility of any predictor variables that have been previously identified in the literature. While the review aims to provide a comprehensive overview of safety profiles and risk stratification, the synthesis is based on pooling data from multiple studies, which is crucial for establishing reliable estimates of event frequency. Understanding these adverse event rates and potential predictors is key for optimizing sedation protocols in the pediatric ED setting.

articles.title: Presepsin for sepsis diagnosis in emergency departments: a multicentre study
articles.takeaway: While presepsin showed marginally better diagnostic accuracy than PCT in this cohort, remember that its utility is supportive, not definitive. Do not let a single biomarker result dictate the need for aggressive resuscitation or antibiotic escalation; always integrate these findings with the patient's clinical picture and lactate trends. The association with increased mortality is worth noting for risk stratification, but clinical judgment remains paramount.
articles.shortSummary: This multicenter study assessed the utility of presepsin and procalcitonin (PCT) for diagnosing sepsis and septic shock in the emergency department setting, using the established Sepsis-3 criteria.
articles.summary: This multicenter study assessed the utility of presepsin and procalcitonin (PCT) for diagnosing sepsis and septic shock in the emergency department setting, using the established Sepsis-3 criteria. The authors found that presepsin demonstrated slightly superior diagnostic accuracy compared to PCT for both sepsis and septic shock, as evidenced by the Area Under the ROC Curve (AUROC) values. Furthermore, elevated levels of both biomarkers were independently associated with increased 30-day mortality risk. While the predictive values are noted, the overall diagnostic accuracy reported for both markers was considered modest. The key conclusion emphasizes that these biomarkers are best utilized as adjuncts to clinical assessment rather than standalone diagnostic tools for guiding critical decisions like resuscitation or antibiotic therapy.

articles.title: Should anticoagulants be initiated in patients with sepsis-induced new-onset atrial fibrillation? Best evidence topic report
articles.takeaway: For patients with sepsis-induced new-onset AF, do not initiate routine anticoagulation based on current observational data, as the evidence does not support stroke prevention and one study noted increased stroke risk. Continue to weigh the benefits against the bleeding risk, but recognize that a definitive randomized trial is needed before changing standard practice. Be mindful of the potential for increased bleeding or stroke, respectively.
articles.shortSummary: This topic report synthesizes the current evidence regarding the use of anticoagulation for stroke prevention in patients who develop new-onset atrial fibrillation secondary to sepsis.
articles.summary: This topic report synthesizes the current evidence regarding the use of anticoagulation for stroke prevention in patients who develop new-onset atrial fibrillation secondary to sepsis. The review examined four observational studies, and the findings were not conclusive enough to support routine anticoagulation. Notably, none of the studies demonstrated a significant reduction in stroke risk with the initiation of anticoagulation, and one large observational cohort even suggested a paradoxical increase in stroke incidence among those receiving anticoagulants. While bleeding risk was not consistently elevated across the literature, one study did point toward a potential benefit of anticoagulation regarding overall mortality. Given these mixed and somewhat conflicting results, the authors stress that a definitive randomized controlled trial is necessary to clarify the appropriate role of anticoagulation in this high-risk, septic population.

articles.title: Validation of The Resuscitation Management (THERM) score in the emergency department
articles.takeaway: When stratifying risk in the resuscitation bay, remember that the THERM score integrates bicarbonate and systolic blood pressure status in a unique way. If you have the opportunity to calculate this score, it appears to offer a predictive edge over standard tools like NEWS2. However, given that this is a validation piece, treat the score as an adjunct to your clinical judgment rather than a standalone determinant for escalation.
articles.shortSummary: This paper focuses on validating the Resuscitation Management (THERM) score, a specific prediction tool designed for critically ill patients presenting in the emergency department.
articles.summary: This paper focuses on validating the Resuscitation Management (THERM) score, a specific prediction tool designed for critically ill patients presenting in the emergency department. The score is calculated using a combination of physiological parameters: adding the bicarbonate level (capped at 22 mmol/L) to the total Glasgow Coma Scale, and then subtracting 4 if the systolic blood pressure is low. The authors report that in their initial validation, THERM demonstrated significantly superior performance compared to the National Early Warning Score (NEWS), even when considering the updated NEWS2 system. This suggests that incorporating bicarbonate and specific blood pressure criteria might offer a more nuanced prognostic capability for resource allocation in the resuscitation bay.

articles.title: A Woman With Rapidly Progressive Weakness
articles.takeaway: When encountering acute, progressive, flaccid paralysis with a distinct sensory level in a patient with CKD, strongly consider a myelopathy or polyneuropathy rather than just a simple motor nerve issue. The combination of flaccidity, areflexia, and sensory loss below a specific dermatome mandates a focused search for underlying metabolic derangements or compressive myelopathy. Don't miss the workup for toxins or electrolyte abnormalities.
articles.shortSummary: This case report details a 57-year-old woman with known chronic kidney disease who presented with a rapid, 6-hour onset of bilateral, nontraumatic lower extremity weakness and numbness.
articles.summary: This case report details a 57-year-old woman with known chronic kidney disease who presented with a rapid, 6-hour onset of bilateral, nontraumatic lower extremity weakness and numbness. On exam, she demonstrated profound flaccid paralysis in the legs, absent reflexes, and a distinct dissociative sensory loss affecting pain and temperature below the nipple-to-xiphoid level, alongside incontinence. The presentation is highly suggestive of an acute neuromuscular process, and the authors highlight the importance of considering underlying systemic or metabolic etiologies in the setting of progressive weakness. The clinical picture warrants a thorough workup to differentiate between peripheral nerve injury, acute myelopathy, or a toxin/metabolic cause, especially given her comorbidities.

articles.title: Methodological limitations of NOM in the WSES acute appendicitis guidelines
articles.takeaway: When considering NOM for acute appendicitis, remember that the diagnostic yield of admission CT is imperfect, potentially missing microperforations or occult neoplasms. Given the high recurrence rate approaching 43%, these limitations suggest that while NOM is an option, it requires careful risk stratification and awareness of the diagnostic gaps inherent in the imaging modality.
articles.shortSummary: This piece critically examines the methodological weaknesses surrounding the use of nonoperative management (NOM) for acute appendicitis, particularly in light of the recent 2025 WSES Guidelines that support its use for…
articles.summary: This piece critically examines the methodological weaknesses surrounding the use of nonoperative management (NOM) for acute appendicitis, particularly in light of the recent 2025 WSES Guidelines that support its use for uncomplicated cases. The authors apply the GRADE framework and highlight that relying on admission computed tomography carries significant limitations. Specifically, CT can severely underestimate the presence of microperforations and has a documented failure rate of up to 3.2% in excluding occult appendiceal neoplasms. Given these diagnostic uncertainties and the reported recurrence rates reaching 42.6%, the article urges a more cautious approach to adopting NOM.

editions.headline: Targeting the Source: Advances in PE and Trauma Resuscitation
editions.summary:
The evidence base continues to refine our approach to high-acuity, multi-system failure. For intermediate-risk pulmonary embolism, the HI-PEITHO trial suggests that while anticoagulation remains foundational, adding ultrasound-guided, catheter-directed fibrinolysis may offer a tangible benefit in preventing short-term hemodynamic collapse in the sickest patients. Similarly, in the trauma bay, the TOWAR trial provides reassuring data suggesting that administering whole, type O blood en route to the hospital is a clinically equivalent and potentially simpler alternative to complex component therapy for managing anticipated hemorrhagic shock.

Beyond the pulmonary and hemorrhagic pathways, procedural efficiency is gaining traction. For routine vascular access in young children, incorporating a liquid crystal vein locator appears to yield measurable improvements in first-attempt success rates. Furthermore, for acute rib fractures, the data supporting the addition of a Serratus Anterior Plane Block to standard analgesia suggests a valuable, readily implementable adjunct for improving pain control scores in the ED setting. These findings, alongside the continued refinement of transfusion triggers in high-risk cardiac patients, underscore a trend toward targeted, procedural, and resource-aware interventions.

This collection demands attention because it moves beyond broad guidelines, offering specific, actionable algorithms—from localized thrombolysis to improved vascular access—that can immediately refine care at the bedside.
editions.notes: Keep an eye on the procedural feasibility and resource implications of these promising, targeted interventions.

Don't change staffing models based on the premise alone; wait for the full results to see if the evidence supports a direct impact on patient death rates. Be cautious about generalizing findings from other care settings to our chaotic ED environment.

The authors synthesized literature published between 2004 and 2025, which is a broad scope for such an intervention. While the general literature suggests that establishing nurse ratios benefits both patients and staff in various care settings, the applicability of these findings to the unique, high-acuity environment of the ED remains uncertain. The primary goal of this review was to assess the direct association between these ratios and ED mortality. However, the provided excerpt only details the methodology—the search strategy across major databases and the independent review process—without presenting the actual findings regarding mortality rates.
